channel.title: NPR Topics: Culture
channel.description: Art and entertainment commentary plus interviews, book reviews, movie reviews, music reviews, comedy, and visual art. Subscribe to podcasts and follow trends in music, painting, art, architecture, photography, and design.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: How to keep an ancient text interesting to students? Make it a summer blockbuster
item.description: English teachers are excited to have a new adaptation of Homer's The Odyssey to use to get students excited about the epic poem.
item.content:encoded:
English teachers are excited to have a new adaptation of Homer's The Odyssey to use to get students excited about the epic poem.

(Image credit: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal Pic/Melinda Sue Gordon)
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Carrillo

item.title: Trump allies are poised to weaken protections for historic sites — not just in D.C.
item.description: A council packed with Trump appointees is poised to rewrite historic preservation rules. Critics say that could help him build his D.C. arch faster — and endanger important sites across the country.
item.content:encoded:
A council packed with Trump appointees is poised to rewrite historic preservation rules. Critics say that could help him build his D.C. arch faster — and endanger important sites across the country.

(Image credit: Jen Golbeck)
item.dc:creator: Rachel Treisman

item.title: Lee Sung Jin talks about the breakthrough that led him to 'BEEF'
item.description: Set in an upscale country club, Season 2 of BEEF has been nominated for 16 Emmys. Lee spent two decades writing other people's shows before he started telling stories inspired by his own life.
item.content:encoded: Set in an upscale country club, Season 2 of BEEF has been nominated for 16 Emmys. Lee spent two decades writing other people's shows before he started telling stories inspired by his own life.
item.dc:creator: Tonya Mosley

item.title: Get into med school in the graphic memoir 'See One, Do One, Teach One'
item.description: Grace Farris' book brings readers behind the med school scenes — documenting anxiety over loans, bouts of impostor syndrome, friendships forged in stress, and the ever-impossible work-life balance.
item.content:encoded: Grace Farris' book brings readers behind the med school scenes — documenting anxiety over loans, bouts of impostor syndrome, friendships forged in stress, and the ever-impossible work-life balance.
item.dc:creator: Tahneer Oksman

item.title: The White House pushes healthy eating. It cut a landmark nutrition education program
item.description: Nutrition advocates and public health experts are mystified by the decision to eliminate SNAP-Ed, given the Trump administration's embrace of the Make America Healthy Again agenda.
item.content:encoded:
Nutrition advocates and public health experts are mystified by the decision to eliminate SNAP-Ed, given the Trump administration's embrace of the Make America Healthy Again agenda.

(Image credit: Rachel Wisniewski for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Joe Hernandez

item.title: 'Lucky' is a crime series that starts mid-caper — and dares you to keep up
item.description: Based on Marissa Stapley's 2021 crime novel, Lucky is an aggressively entertaining series about a con woman who absconds with millions of dollars — then must evade the mob and the law.
item.content:encoded: Based on Marissa Stapley's 2021 crime novel, Lucky is an aggressively entertaining series about a con woman who absconds with millions of dollars — then must evade the mob and the law.
item.dc:creator: David Bianculli

item.title: In pursuit of 'Instagram face,' are we losing the imperfections that make us human?
item.description: Plastic surgery is becoming so normalized and undetectable, it's changing our relationship to reality. The New Yorker staff writer Jia Tolentino considers how beauty standards have dovetailed with AI.
item.content:encoded: Plastic surgery is becoming so normalized and undetectable, it's changing our relationship to reality. The New Yorker staff writer Jia Tolentino considers how beauty standards have dovetailed with AI.
item.dc:creator: Tonya Mosley

item.title: People are watching the Reflecting Pool like reality TV. What does that say about us?
item.description: It's not just watching paint dry. The twists and turns of the Reflecting Pool repairs, originally a two-week project, have kept bloggers and viewers busy all summer.
item.content:encoded:
It's not just watching paint dry. The twists and turns of the Reflecting Pool repairs, originally a two-week project, have kept bloggers and viewers busy all summer.

(Image credit: Jackie Lay)
item.dc:creator: Rachel Treisman

item.title: Smithsonian too focused on 'oppressors and the oppressed,' say House Republicans
item.description: In a House DOGE subcommittee hearing, GOP representatives excoriated the director of the National Museum of American History, following a White House report earlier this month that also criticized her.
item.content:encoded:
In a House DOGE subcommittee hearing, GOP representatives excoriated the director of the National Museum of American History, following a White House report earlier this month that also criticized her.

(Image credit: Andrew Harnik)
item.dc:creator: Anastasia Tsioulcas

item.title: Broadway legend André De Shields helps bring 'Hadestown' to the big screen
item.description: De Shields made his Broadway debut in the 1975 original production of The Wiz. He won a Tony in 2019 for his performance in Hadestown and co-stars in the new film of last year's London revival.
item.content:encoded: De Shields made his Broadway debut in the 1975 original production of The Wiz. He won a Tony in 2019 for his performance in Hadestown and co-stars in the new film of last year's London revival.
item.dc:creator: Terry Gross
